title: Gaslighting: Recognize Manipulative and Emotionally Abusive People—and Break Free
author: Samantha Parker

chapters.title: I'm sorry, but not really
chapters.text:
"I'm sorry you felt that way." You've probably heard that one at some point in your business or personal life, probably both. It was a pretty innocent phrase and presumably an apology, but has it ever made you feel good and relieved? If not, rest assured; nothing is wrong with you. Your feelings just weren't validated, and the person who said this to you is a gaslighter who didn't want to take responsibility for their wrongdoings.

A gaslighter aims to shatter your perception of reality and make you question your feelings.

However, the "conditional apology" is not the only weapon in the gaslighter's arsenal. Among other tricks are:
• Triangulation: A gaslighter never confronts you directly. Instead, they go to your mutuals and send their message to you through them.
• Splitting: A gaslighter tries to pit you against your friends, family, and colleagues, watching from a distance as the chaos in your communication unfolds.
• Complisulting: Gaslighters always give compliments that seem like passive-aggressive insults, so-called "complisults."

Do you recognize these qualities in a family member, colleague, loved one, or even yourself? Are you ready to end the cycle of manipulation and restore joy in your life? Fear not; this summary is here to help you spot even the subtlest signs of manipulation. Stick around, and at the end, you'll find a handy Try this guide to help you set boundaries and keep your inner peace!

chapters.title: All that glitters is not love
chapters.text:
Dating a gaslighter is a wild emotional rollercoaster that doesn't seem to end. At first, your gaslighter will love-bomb you — shower you with praise and compliments and bombard you with so much love and affection that it is hard to resist. But the moment you get comfortable in your relationship, they will turn to stonewalling. They will abruptly ignore your messages and calls and avoid you altogether. At this point, you wonder what might have caused this behavior. You'll even ask yourself, "Is there something wrong with me?”

After your gaslighting partner has been completely silent, you might want to give up on this relationship and stop calling them. And just like in similar scenarios, this is when your gaslighter will roll back in and try to hoover you back into the relationship. They will do this by making empty promises or reminding you about stuff you left in their flat. It seems like everything will get on the right track, doesn't it? But it won't; it is a delusion — the gaslighting will cycle repeatedly.

Knowing these people are out there is quite unnerving, isn't it? And you probably are asking, "Are there any distinct signs that the person I am talking to (or dating) will turn into a gaslighter?" Yes, absolutely! Here are some things to look out for:

• They like to talk only about themselves and ask zero questions about you.
• They will talk about getting married and having children at the beginning of your relationship.
• They don't use basic manners and treat people you care about badly.
• They mention they cheated in their last relationship.
• They talk badly about their previous partners.
• They brag about their work, houses, cars, and fancy travels, but confirming this information is difficult.
• They mention how many dating options they have but still choose you.
• They drop the names of famous people they allegedly know just to impress you.
• They do not respect your personal space.

While confidence can be attractive, excessive charm may mask deeper, even sinister, motives.

Did you know? Many think that gaslighters are primarily men, but this behavior is not gender specific. The reason this stereotype exists is that some men are reluctant to talk about how women gaslighted them. The term "gaslighting" gained popularity after the 1944 film Gaslight, where a husband tries to convince his wife that she is going crazy.

chapters.title: Gaslightproof your career
chapters.text:
Even the most ideal and carefree job may become hell if you work alongside a gaslighter. All your hard work could be in vain when a gaslighter tries to take credit for it. Your shining achievements can suddenly become dim when a gaslighter attempts to diminish them. All your peace can disappear with a gaslighter's sabotage, bullying, and harassment.

Harassment is about power. It is about "keeping you in your place." ~ Stephanie Moulton Sarkis, PhD

Stephanie Moulton

In the case of gaslighters, the rule of thumb is to keep yourself as far away from them as feasible. However, if distancing is impossible, here are some ways you can protect yourself:
1. Avoid being alone with a gaslighter: If you have a meeting with a gaslighting colleague, invite someone from your team. Gaslighters will be more hesitant to cause you harm when there is an observer. But if they show their true colors, you will have a witness to confirm your words.
2. Meet your boss once a week: Keep written notes about your work and update your supervisor about your progress regularly. If someone tries to claim your accomplishments, you will have proof of the truth.
3. Refrain from drinking at office parties: Alcohol will make you vulnerable, and gaslighters will take advantage of this situation to victimize you, lie about you, or attack you. What's worse, under the influence, you might gossip about or even confront the gaslighter, neither of which will play out well for you.
4. Document everything: Keep notes of every interaction you had with the gaslighter down to the smallest detail: date, time, witnesses, and who said what. Also, save this information on your personal device with password protection.
5. Change your workplace: One of the best ways to deal with gaslighters is to distance yourself from them. Find another job elsewhere or request a department transfer if you like your company and its culture.

Seek feedback from trusted colleagues to foster support and reduce a gaslighter's impact on your contributions.

chapters.title: How politicians pull the strings
chapters.text:
Politics attracts different people with varying agendas. While one strives to serve others and change the world, another only seeks attention and recognition for malicious purposes. Gaslighting is a common strategy for manipulating the public's opinion, and here is how you can recognize it in politics:

• They act as if they are almighty.
Gaslighters in politics want to show people they have all the power in the world and that everyone must submit to this power.

• They don't take responsibility for their actions.
If something terrible happens, gaslighters will blame anyone — their opponents, staff, or external circumstances beyond their control. They need this facade of innocence to continue manipulating and influencing others.

• They hate smart people.
Intellectuals pose a significant danger to gaslighters, as only they can catch their lies and confront them with solid facts and arguments. To be challenged is a gaslighter's greatest fear.

• They turn people against marginalized groups.
Gaslighters look for the public enemy and the origin of all misfortunes in the country, and marginalized people become their scapegoats. They then try to instill fear and resentment in voters, which not only distracts them from the real causes of the issue but also breeds stigma and unhealthy stereotypes.

• They use questionable sources of information.
Gaslighters like propaganda — the false information aimed to hurt their opponents or the disenfranchised. Misleading narratives help gaslighters distort reality, spread hatred and fear, and dominate conversations.

Set limits on the information you take in while online — getting lost in nonstop negative news can take a toll on you.

Politics is something that few people enjoy dabbling about. But remember: every voice and every opinion counts, and you can oppose political gaslighting on a personal level. In fact, it's pretty simple. Take every piece of information from political debates, news outlets, and social media with a grain of salt. These criteria can keep you from falling down a political rabbit hole:

1. What is the source of the story? Does its URL seem legit?
2. Is there any correlation with other news sources? Do they all report the same viewpoint?
3. Does the story have an attributed author?
4. Does the story quote eligible sources and studies?

See how you've already become stronger and more resilient against gaslighting? Keep going — even more actionable steps are just ahead.

chapters.title: The cost of belonging
chapters.text:
There is no place where you can question your reality more than in cults and closed communities. Cult members will keep you in their tight grip, isolate you from the outside world, and use gaslighting to make you think their ideals are the norm. Cult-like mentality ranges widely, from religious communities to political and terrorist extremist groups. Here are just a few examples of cult-like mentality:

• You no longer have free will in your life.
• You have to obey a leader; questioning his authority is equivalent to committing a crime.
• There is physical or psychological punishment for disobedience.
• Cult members force you to give your money and possessions to the community.
• They see science and mental health awareness as something wrong.
• You must follow strict rules; they might concern your clothes, behavior, interaction with others, family relationships, and work.
• If you leave the group, you might get stalked or harassed. Also, your family members and friends who stay in the cult cannot contact you.
• You must use unique jargon within the cult.
• The group can decide what spouse you'll get and what upbringing your children will receive.

More time, energy, devotion, and money to the group, the more "special" you become. ~ Stephanie Moulton Sarkis, PhD

Stephanie Moulton

Getting out of a cult is a risky endeavor. No one has to know about your exit strategy if you have one. Also, if you try to reach outside communities for help, you must be careful about all the calls and texts you make — other cult members might monitor them. Once you are out, it might not be the end of the story. If you suspect the cult has set up surveillance on you, immediately inform law enforcement.

Being in a cult can leave a profound mark on mental health, causing anxiety, depression, and feelings of worthlessness, and counseling is the most logical step forward. In therapy, you can find a safe and supportive environment to explore your experience and reclaim your sense of self.

Prioritizing your well-being is essential, not selfish. Only when you feel safe can you thrive.

Please note that the information in this key point is for general guidance only and is not professional advice. If you or someone you know is in a cult or feels unsafe, seek help from professionals or law enforcement.

chapters.title: Mirror, mirror on the wall
chapters.text:
Did you happen to recognize some of these gaslighting techniques? But not in your friends' stories or social media sensations, but in your own behavior? Relax; it's ok; you are likely not a gaslighter if you are self-aware enough to admit it. True gaslighters will never assume they are problematic and will never seek help. If you suspect yourself, perhaps you just have gaslighting traits, such as:
• You have a short temper.
• You "black out" when angry and don't remember what you've said or done.
• You expect people to do what you want but rarely communicate your needs.
• You lie, even in cases when lying is not necessary.
• Your tone is mostly sarcastic.
• You see other people as selfish.

Think about your past surroundings. If your parents or caregivers were toxic and gaslighted you throughout your childhood, then your gaslighting traits are nothing but the self-preservation mechanisms you used to protect yourself. And because of these experiences, establishing healthy relationships might be complex. The good news is that you can shake off this burden. Here are some helpful tips:

1. Address your concerns and needs when they arise: Replace "you" statements with "I feel" sentences. For example, "I feel very frustrated when you miss my calls" instead of "You always miss my calls." This way, you will not trigger another person's defensive mode.
2. Talk as equals: In communication, you can choose from three models of behavior — parent, adult, and child. In parent mode, you talk down to people and use expressions like "you must" and "you should" as if you are a parent talking to your kids. In child mode, you get emotional: crying or getting angry. However, for healthy communication, you should get into adult mode — listen carefully to what the other person has to say and calmly explain your motivation. You can even agree to disagree, but you do that with respect.
3. Match your communication to your body language: Maintaining eye contact and using open hand gestures can reinforce your sincerity and engagement. On the other hand, crossed arms and looking around the room indicate uneasiness, disinterest, or complete boredom.

We all have traits formed by our past, but how we choose to grow is what defines us.

chapters.title: Try this
chapters.text:
Ready to stop second-guessing yourself and take back control? These simple yet powerful steps will help you navigate gaslighting and rediscover your strength.

1. Distance yourself from the gaslighter (if possible): Limit their access to your time by declining calls, minimizing texts, or keeping conversations short and focused.
2. Define and reinforce your boundaries: Write down three non-negotiable boundaries, such as "I won't tolerate someone invalidating my feelings" or "I will end conversations where I'm being manipulated." Stick to this agenda in your communication with others.
3. Start a "reality journal": Dedicate five minutes daily to documenting key events and conversations and how they made you feel. Be specific — include dates, times, and any witnesses, if possible.
4. Invest in professional support: Seek guidance from a therapist who specializes in trauma or emotional abuse to help you process your experiences.
5. Lean on allies: Build a support system by identifying two or three people you trust who can help you reality-check what's happening. Reach out to them when you feel unsure or overwhelmed.
6. Call it what it is: Mentally acknowledge gaslighting when you notice it happening by saying to yourself: "This is gaslighting, not my reality."

You have everything it takes to stand firm in your truth. Trust your instincts, honor your feelings, and remember that your reality is valid, no matter who tries to rewrite it.

Read it till the end?
